exercises.title: Replace Magic Numbers with Constants
exercises.description: Magic numbers make code unclear. Replace the hardcoded numbers with named constants, then run the same calculation.
exercises.starterCode:
# TODO: Define these constants at the top
# SALES_TAX_RATE = 0.085
# LOYALTY_DISCOUNT = 0.10
# FREE_SHIPPING_THRESHOLD = 50.0
SALES_TAX_RATE = 0.085
LOYALTY_DISCOUNT = 0.10
FREE_SHIPPING_THRESHOLD = 50.0

# Calculation using constants (do not change these lines)
cart_total = 75.00

discount_amount = cart_total * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT
price_after_discount = cart_total - discount_amount
tax_amount = price_after_discount * SALES_TAX_RATE
final_total = price_after_discount + tax_amount
shipping = 0.0 if cart_total >= FREE_SHIPPING_THRESHOLD else 5.99

print(f"Cart total:    ${cart_total:.2f}")
print(f"Discount:      -${discount_amount:.2f}")
print(f"Tax:           +${tax_amount:.2f}")
print(f"Shipping:      ${shipping:.2f}")
print(f"Final total:   ${final_total + shipping:.2f}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Cart total:    $75.00
Discount:      -$7.50
Tax:           +$5.74
Shipping:      $0.00
Final total:   $73.24
hints: The calculation lines already use the constant names — just define the constants above.

exercises.title: Naming Convention Practice
exercises.description: Identify and apply the correct naming convention for each identifier: snake_case for variables/functions, ALL_CAPS for constants, and CapWords for classes (not needed here).
exercises.starterCode:
# Each variable below uses the WRONG naming style.
# Rewrite each one with the CORRECT Python convention,
# then print the same values.

# Bad: camelCase variable — fix to snake_case
studentFirstName = "Alice"  # fix this
student_first_name = "Alice"
print(student_first_name)

# Bad: ALL_CAPS variable (should only be for constants) — fix to snake_case
CURRENT_SCORE = 87   # this should be a regular variable
current_score = 87
print(current_score)

# Bad: should be a constant (never changes), but written in snake_case
days_per_week = 7    # fix to ALL_CAPS constant
DAYS_PER_WEEK = 7
print(DAYS_PER_WEEK)
hints: Variables that change: use snake_case (e.g., student_first_name).
Values that never change (like days in a week): use ALL_CAPS (DAYS_PER_WEEK).

exercises.title: Self-Documenting Code
exercises.description: Rewrite the calculation using descriptive variable names and named constants so the code explains itself without needing comments.
exercises.starterCode:
# Original code (cryptic names):
# p = 1000
# r = 0.05
# n = 12
# t = 3
# a = p * (1 + r/n) ** (n*t)
# print(a)

# TODO: Rewrite with descriptive names and constants
PRINCIPAL = 1000.0          # initial deposit
ANNUAL_RATE = 0.05           # 5% annual interest rate
COMPOUNDINGS_PER_YEAR = 12   # monthly compounding
years = 3

final_amount = PRINCIPAL * (1 + ANNUAL_RATE / COMPOUNDINGS_PER_YEAR) ** (COMPOUNDINGS_PER_YEAR * years)

print(f"Principal:    ${PRINCIPAL:.2f}")
print(f"Rate:          {ANNUAL_RATE * 100:.1f}% per year")
print(f"Years:         {years}")
print(f"Final amount: ${final_amount:.2f}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Principal:    $1000.00
Rate:          5.0% per year
Years:         3
Final amount: $1161.47
hints: Use descriptive names: PRINCIPAL instead of p, ANNUAL_RATE instead of r.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Naming Conventions
warmup.description: Python uses snake_case for variables and ALL_CAPS for constants. What does this print?
warmup.starterCode:
# Constants (never change)
MAX_SPEED = 120
PI = 3.14159

# Variables (can change)
current_speed = 80
radius = 5

circle_area = PI * radius ** 2
print(f"Max speed: {MAX_SPEED}")
print(f"Circle area: {circle_area:.2f}")
